transcript.text: I heard his name before, yeah.
Natty?
Yes, I was trying to remember the name, but couldn't.
Same one, yes, Same one.
Anyway, it's cool.
Well, great to connect.
Would love to hear a little bit more.
I had you reaching out.
They had shared some context in the email a little bit, but I can just do a quick background on myself.
Really?
I can go through all sorts of different things today.
would be helpful just to get an understanding of kind of where your mind's at and calibrate me on that.
But so Kyle Gaudreau, VP, customer growth over here.
I've worn a lot of different hats here in my time at GrowthX.
I'm leading on the sales side now.
I'm sure in a couple of weeks I'll be doing other things.
I was leading the client services side of the business most of my tenure here.
Just over a year ago, I actually was the first customer of GrowthX.
So I've seen all sorts of different sides of the journey.
It's been a wild ride.
My background's more in digital and growth.
A lot of experience there, 19 years.
I before GrowthX, a lot of different stuff on the agency side, but then was in-house for almost 10 years.
Brands would usually hire me because they'd want to be less reliant on paid search, diversify their channels, help stand up experimentation engines.
Organic usually was a big focus of that.
And so it brought me to places like Anaplan, Trey, Sendoso, most recently Homebase, where I was leading growth and met Marcel, our CEO and co-founder.
And he was a CMO at DeepGram at the time.
He was doing some workshops of some fun AI workflow things he was building.
And I was like, oh, man, this is so cool.
This is like building.
I wanted to do an experiment with.
And then the week after, I was like, wait, I don't have time to build these workflows.
Can you please help me build these workflows?
Somehow we found time to do that.
And we just, you know, it early days of AI content and experimentation.
Like nothing had been proven at that point that you could drive growth other than, you know, things Marcel had seen, obviously, but still early days.
And, you know, it started to.

Really have success at Homebase, brands like Ramp and Reddit, a lot of also like very early stage customers, they need to develop their content engine and put a lot of time and effort in just thinking about like, well, it's the right structure of the team.
What is the right way to structure processes?
Like, how do you need to evolve these workflows?
And so I'm a bit biased, but I can confidently say I don't think anyone has put as much time and effort in thinking how to scale out high quality AI content as us.
And we're finding it's resonating with a lot of folks.
There's a lot of people who need content.
It's really hard to hire content teams and coordinate them.
And we have a lot of experience building those engines ourselves.
And so essentially think of us as like we have platform side of the house.
We've largely built that for our internal team's purposes.
And we're really providing the end to end building of strategy, executing, creating the things like imagery that's necessary for this content.
We stage and publish it and just like.
The things end-to-end, as former in-house marketers felt like was lacking.
And so it's a bit of a high level.
Of course, can go into more context, but hopefully that's helpful.
Yeah, it is.
So I'll try to give you some background of myself.
I own a group of companies.
It's in one side, I have agencies, digital marketing agency, data agency, and even headhunting agency here in Israel.
We are the largest in Israel, like private, which is not related or owned by some big companies.
So we are the largest.
And on the other side, so there I have like CEOs and I have management.
And on the other side, they built a venture building.
Do you know the term?
Venture builder?
Yeah.
I don't know if I've heard it in that way, but I could be presumptuous of maybe the intent behind it.
I think that in US they call it venture studio or factory.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
There are different meanings.
Yeah.
So I built a venture builder here.
We have access to the best tech talent here in Israel that are coming out of the military.
And I started to build from e-commerce companies.
I own a company called Tanao, which has built the smallest Bible in the world in nanotechnology.
And it's engraved in jewelry.
And it's doing great.
And from the other side, like deep tech companies.
And I saw your activity and I was impressed.
And I love the way you think and the way you lead.
It's something that I really relate to this way of thinking.
And I just wanted to explore and to understand if I can use it to my companies, if I can use it for the agency.
So it's pretty open.
I don't have like a specific agenda here.
But I would love to explore and understand more in order to understand how we can work together.
Yeah, absolutely.
Great context, very interesting set of different hats you're wearing there.
Yeah, yeah, I do.
Keeps that everyday fresh, I'm sure.
Very interesting, yes.
And exhausting.
I can imagine.
I don't know, you know, sounds like you're quite ambitious.
So you're finding that energy somewhere.
So, you know, sounds fun.
But yeah, you know, happy to dive in a bit how we work, you know, think of it as like, ultimately, like how we want a lot of our customers to think and operate is to a bit more like publishers.
And what we have found is like that's starting to become more and more of a popularized thing in the SaaS space of people are realizing the value behind that.

We don't work just with SaaS brands, to be clear, but especially finding that resonating with a lot of SaaS brands, because a lot of the mindset that goes into that is like creating truly high quality content that is going to be valuable for your ICP.
And so historically, things that have been focused on, say, SEO have been, it had a bit of stigma behind them.
And rightfully so at times of like being.
Less than content, being highly just focused on pure SEO and not being great content overall.
That is nowhere the place we want our customers to be.
We wouldn't categorize ourselves as an SEO shop.
We really want to specialize more in helping build high-quality content engines for our customers, right?
The way we think about it is how can we really, really get into understanding your personas at a really, really deep level?
Like what motivates them, their jobs to be done, et cetera?
There is naturally going to be a set of rich questions that they're going to have at varying phases of their journey associated to all those things.

And the best that we can create, truly great, engaging content that answers those questions, that will lift SEO, that will lift GEO, or whatever acronym you want to use that's happening in generative today.
We lean on GEO.
That we're finding we're getting traction across the board with that.
Like are there particular things that you need to think about and optimize in best practices?
Of course, but just generally a

A lot of what we found is doing a lot of, you know, laying that right foundation from a strategic perspective, doing a lot of context engineering into how do we extract knowledge from you, apply that knowledge, and educate our team, and then bring that forward into our AI workflows.
A lot of what we've refined is how to make that mix work, how to extract that knowledge, how to provide that broader context that leads to repeatable outcomes from AI.
And, you know, a lot of that has been just in form, because we've taken on a lot of, like, quite technical accounts in our journey.
It stressed me out a little bit early on when we do this, to be honest, but I think it really helped harden us and helped to refine our process and adapt, right?

And so, you know, we've had so many brands where we're speaking to an engineering audience, and, you they're going to be very specific of what they're going to care about, what's going to resonate with them.
You know, say something just a little bit wrong, it's immediately, you're going to lose their trust, right?
We have other brands where we have to pass legal review.
We're working with one right now.
Literally every piece of content we develop has to be reviewed by a doctor.
And so, like, we've developed fact-checking workflows and other processes that help us out.
But happy to jump in a little bit.
Oh, my tab's got a little messed up.
Let me just fix this real quick.
And just share a bit of how we think and work.
And then I can show you a little bit of, like, our platform.
So, essentially, you know, think of it as, like, we have worked with a lot of brands at different phases.
We've experimented with a lot of different things.
You know, but what we've found is the vast majority of time, regardless of phase, a lot of brands need a lot of help into really refining this editorial layer first and foremost.
And so, think of these as varying work streams that we can do.
But it's quite rare we wouldn't start here.
Thank you.
For brands we work with.

Reason being is like, there's just so much important coordination that goes into dialing in things like tone, voice, structure, just really refining who we're speaking to, what that looks and feels like.
What are those important topics?
Are those topics resonating?
We find even like teams that have product marketers and things of that nature, they're still not getting to the right depth, ideally, to lay this foundation.
And so we like starting here because it helps us understand that at a deep level.
There's a lot of growth as you get those signals and can continue to grow this engine, just get more qualified eyeballs to your website.
And then naturally, though, that starts to open up paths of distribution and conversion.
That could be, you know, things experimenting around social or email or, you know, creating lead magnets.

That would be the core focus only.
And that's where the starting place would be.
We really want to start with building this longer form content engine.
This could be blogs, by the way, but not limited to blogs.
And we just find this opens up a lot of different paths for us.
And so essentially, that's really informed the team we've built.
This has been one of the cool things for me, just seeing the journey in the way I have.
We've been able to, you know, we're not a typical agency operating on typical agency margins.
And so we're able to, we're really have found, like, the people are really key to the success and also has resonated quite well with our customers.
And so we build a highly editorially focused team.
Matthew Panzarino is our chief content officer.
He was editor chief of TechCrunch for over 10 years, you know, really running a lot of that business.
Then we have a layer of managing directors.
This is where, like, a lot of that strategy lives.
Folks with, like, 20-plus years of SEO and growth experience help it.
People who have helped build, like, G2, ClickUp, Fanatic, StripAdvisor.
We just have seen a ton of success.
And then common in these kind of like editorially publisher-focused teams is managing editors.

And so that is a key person for all our engagements that is like hands-on developing the content, getting that feedback, continuing to refine all those things around just getting to a repeatable layer of quality.
So Sydney was lead editor at SEMrush.
And point being, we just have built a pretty badass team and have found that's really helped a lot.
And I can show you a little bit of like how we structure this.
But essentially, we structure our engagements in two phases.
The strategy sprint typically starts around 20 to 30K.
It's six to eight weeks.

Essentially, what we are doing is developing a lot of the necessary, all those things that go into the context engineering I was mentioning, developing a lot of those AI-ready artifacts, dialing in voice and tone and things of that nature, developing a roadmap, a strategy, essentially, and then establishing that.
What we find is just being able to start there and layering that is what enables us to go super fast and get to repeatable outcomes confidently.
Because, you know, speed is a bit of our superpower, but the key is like, how do you make sure you can go fast confidently and get to repeatable outputs?

And so, you know, if we rush past this and we provide high-level context to workflows and a keyword we're talking about, we want to write about, that's probably some version of how most people are working with AI content and dabbling with some other things and getting to pretty thin, just okay content.
The differentiator is all this stuff that we feed into it that we have found is able to help us like really get to a far higher level in terms of quality.
And once we establish that, essentially, we can go to this growth execution phase.
This typically is around six to 12 months, and this is where we are continuing to evolve that strategy, but like hitting the gas, getting to daily publishing and what amount of content.
Content is needed for each brand is quite a bit different.
And that bar that needs to be hit is quite a bit different.
And so we're really defining all this and scoping it, if you will, and just making sure we have high conviction of being successful.
You can always opt out of this phase, take the strategy, take the artifacts, et cetera.
We actually had 100% conversion from this to the execution phase since we've been doing this because brands are just seeing so much value.
I should note, by the way, we have one brand we're working with that's in the sprint phase right now.
We've already created 23 pieces of content for them at week six and published them.
And so it's not like it's just purely strategy.
We are really just trying to figure out how do we get to a repeatable layer.
And then from here, it could be, okay, we feel confident we need to go after these topics, these opportunities with you.
You need something in the range of like 10 articles a week.

Typical range here would be like around like nine to 25K.
When you're on the high end.

also has the benefit of us finding different ways of extracting knowledge from you, getting feedback, finding those different patterns.

So, you know, we, on the SEO side, are going to be leveraging things like Google Search Console, your Google Analytics, and just trying to monitor how fast you're getting indexed, how much engagement are you getting, is that growing?
On the GEO side, we partner with Scrunch.
We've worked with a lot of different, and explored a lot of these different tools.
We find they're all largely the same.
Essentially, they are just creating different prompts, and then pinning through the API to perplexity, chat to PT, et cetera, and just looking at citations and placement, et cetera.

So our work is really about helping you define through our approach in our process to what are those type of prompts and questions that we should be monitoring, and continuing to refine that over time.
And then we can essentially see, like, how is this shaping versus your competition?
Is this, is there anything worth noticing that you're lacking?
where your competition has traction and you don't, that can inform our roadmap of content that we execute.

Like, are people actually clicking these citations?
What's happening afterwards?

How are you measuring the effect of that on your other channels, like your direct traffic, your brand search, et cetera?
